key_insights.insight: Breathing dysfunction is highly prevalent, with estimates suggesting that 95-99% of people breathe dysfunctionally.
key_insights.quote: Respiratory therapists who do this all day long to elite trainers of Olympians, they say 95 to 99% of the people that they see are breathing dysfunctionally.

key_insights.insight: Modern diseases such as diabetes, asthma, metabolic and autoimmune issues, anxiety and ADHD can be related to poor breathing habits, especially at night.
key_insights.quote: Experts said it is 100% related to your breathing at night, especially.

key_insights.insight: Modern facial structure, influenced by industrialized food, contributes to breathing problems.
key_insights.quote: We can trace the exact point that our facial structure started growing in this deformed way to make us less susceptible to healthy breathing, that changed in such a way to make us such poor breathers. And that point is right when industrialized food came into different cultures.

key_insights.insight: Nasal breathing offers numerous benefits over mouth breathing, including filtering air, capturing moisture, and producing nitric oxide.
key_insights.quote: There are 30 different functions that the nose does for breathing. So not only does it help filter air out and heat air, but it helps capture moisture, about 40% more moisture.

key_insights.insight: Indoor CO2 levels significantly impact cognitive function, with high levels leading to a considerable decline in test results.
key_insights.quote: In one study of 24 employees cognitive scores were 50 percent lower when the participants were exposed to 1400 ppm of CO2 compared with 550 ppm during a working day

key_insights.insight: ADHD in children is often related to sleep-disordered breathing, and fixing breathing habits can resolve the condition.
key_insights.quote: They said there is no such thing as ADHD. What that is is sleep disordered breathing, period. Really? They said it is 100% related to your breathing at night especially

key_insights.insight: Lung capacity is a significant indicator of lifespan, and maintaining or increasing lung capacity can contribute to longevity.
key_insights.quote: Numerous studies have found that the healthier and larger your lungs are the longer you will live that is the greatest indicator of lifespan was lung size and lung health

key_insights.insight: Chronic stress leads to dysfunctional breathing patterns, causing a cycle of further stress and health problems.
key_insights.quote: Researchers have different names for this. They call it email apnea or continuous awareness. I mean, there's an academic name for it, partial attention syndrome. I prefer email apnea, easier to remember. No matter what you call it, it's the idea that when you're in the office place, you're breathing dysfunctionally because of this constant stress loop.

key_insights.insight: Vigorous breathwork techniques, when approached correctly, can help manage stress, and trauma by deliberately stressing the body and learning to recover.
key_insights.quote: You are stressing your body out. And then you're learning how to use your breath to calm your body down

actionable_advice.advice: Practice nasal breathing during exercise and daily activities.
actionable_advice.how_to: Consciously focus on breathing in and out through your nose, even during strenuous activities. If you mouth-breathe during exercise, slow down until you can nose-breathe.

actionable_advice.advice: Implement a standing desk and take frequent walks during work hours.
actionable_advice.how_to: Use a convertible standing desk to alternate between sitting and standing. Set a timer to remind you to take a 10-minute walk every couple of hours.

actionable_advice.advice: Practice diaphragmatic breathing to improve lung capacity and reduce stress.
actionable_advice.how_to: Place your hands above your hip bones or fingers on your collarbone to monitor the movement of your diaphragm and chest as you breathe deeply.

actionable_advice.advice: Hum for 10 minutes four times a day to increase nitric oxide production.
actionable_advice.how_to: Pick a song you enjoy and hum it, feeling the vibration in your nose.

actionable_advice.advice: Address stress throughout the day with double inhales followed by a long exhale
actionable_advice.how_to: When feeling stressed, take two inhales through the nose, and then a slow long exhale.

actionable_advice.advice: Monitor your breath-hold time to assess your respiratory function and CO2 tolerance.
actionable_advice.how_to: Exhale to neutral, time how long you can comfortably hold your breath before the first sign of discomfort.

actionable_advice.advice: Sleep with mouth tape for consistent nasal breathing.
actionable_advice.how_to: Use myotape at night to help the mouth remain closed during sleep to ensure continuous nasal breathing.

actionable_advice.advice: Incorporate foods that require more chewing into your diet.
actionable_advice.how_to: Prioritize whole, unprocessed foods that require chewing, such as raw vegetables and dense meats.

memorable_stories: Nestor's personal experience of resolving respiratory issues through breathwork after years of struggling with bronchitis and mild pneumonia.
The story of observing freedivers diving to incredible depths on a single breath, sparking his interest in the limits of human breathing.
Nestor's self-funded experiment where he and another researcher plugged their noses for 10 days to experience the effects of mouth breathing, which he described as "awful."
The anecdote of a three-year-old with asthma undergoing adenoid and tonsil removal without addressing the underlying breathing dysfunction.
contrarian_claims: The claim that ADHD is primarily a consequence of sleep-disordered breathing, challenging the conventional view of it as a neurological disorder.
The idea that masks do not protect people from viruses and bacteria because of high CO2 indoor environments and poor use of masks.
The suggestion that asthma and anxiety may be reversible through breathing practices rather than simply manageable with medication.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - The Breathing Expert: Breathing Through Your Mouth Can Cause ADHD, Diabetes & Child Sickness! (Must Listen): James Nestor
